compiled_state.title: As a developer, I can trust that onboarding detection results are verified against the filesystem before being presented
compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body:
## Description

**What**: Ground project-detect.sh and detect-stack.sh in filesystem verification using Chain-of-Verification (CoVe) pattern
**Why**: Current detection scripts infer capabilities without confirming they exist, leading to false suggestions (e.g., suggesting make test when no such target exists, missing Docker-based databases)
**Scope**:
- IN: project-detect.sh verification of all detected commands/tools, detect-stack.sh verification, Docker/database detection improvements, confidence tagging (confirmed vs inferred)
- OUT: New detection capabilities beyond what current scripts detect; changes to the onboarding conversation flow

## Done Definitions

- When this story is complete, project-detect.sh and detect-stack.sh verify every command, tool, and target exists on disk before suggesting it (e.g., checking Makefile for actual targets, verifying npm scripts exist in package.json)
  ← Satisfies: "Detection is grounded in filesystem verification"
- When this story is complete, Docker-based database references in docker-compose.yaml, Dockerfile, and application config/code are detected as database presence
  ← Satisfies: "database detection accounts for Docker-based databases referenced in config and code"
- When this story is complete, each detection finding is tagged as confirmed (verified on disk) or inferred (pattern-matched but unverified), and the output format distinguishes the two
  ← Satisfies: "inferred-but-unverified findings are tagged as such and presented differently to the user"
- When this story is complete, unit tests written and passing for all new or modified logic

## Considerations

- [Reliability] Must handle edge cases: missing files, permission errors, ambiguous project structures (e.g., multiple package managers)
- [Testing] Detection verification is deterministic and testable — mock filesystem fixtures can validate CoVe behavior

## Escalation Policy

**Escalation policy**: Proceed unless a significant assumption is required to continue — one that could send the implementation in the wrong direction. Escalate only when genuinely blocked without a reasonable inference. Document all assumptions made without escalating.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body: All 6 implementation tasks closed. Story complete: detection grounded in CoVe, Docker DB detection enhanced, confidence tagging implemented.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart
